payload.answer: Store the 500-row CSV as a structured attachment/index reference for retrieval and querying. Save durable memory only for schema-level metadata and a compact, provenance-linked summary (e.g., deduplicated keys/aggregates). Do not commit every individual row as durable memory unless and until specific rows are confirmed as personally relevant.
payload.decision: Store the dataset as a structured attachment/index reference rather than as individual durable memory items; normalize only schema-level metadata (columns, types) and persist a small, query-relevant summary or deduplicated keys with provenance. For row-level retrieval, keep an ephemeral or retrievable external reference (e.g., file/attachment + search index) instead of committing every row to durable memory.

entity_resolution.reason: No specific entity values from the table (ids/names/values) are provided; only the table structure/scale is available, so resolution to concrete entities is not possible.

payload.intent: Determine the appropriate storage strategy for a large CSV table (500 rows, repeated columns: id, name, value) in Brain (a personal memory system).

relationships.object: large tabular dataset as attachment/index reference
relationships.subject: Brain memory system

memory_cards.statement: For large tabular inputs (e.g., ~500 rows) with repetitive structure, avoid committing each row as separate durable memory; instead, store the table as a structured attachment or indexed reference for retrieval/query.

Persist durable memory at the level of schema and key metadata (e.g., column names and intended types) plus a compact summary (e.g., deduplicated id/name keys or aggregates), with provenance pointing to the source table.

If row-level access is required later, handle it via query against the indexed attachment/external reference (or reconstructed view) rather than storing every row as durable cards; only promote specific rows to durable memory when they become explicitly relevant.

details: No row-level facts were asserted; strategy is based on table scale/structure described in the fixture context.
Decision emphasizes attachment/index approach to avoid durable-memory bloat.

repair_options: If the system requires durable recall of specific rows, promote only a small set of explicitly relevant rows into durable memory with provenance.
If deduplication is possible, store a deduplicated mapping of (id -> name, value summary) rather than all raw rows.
If queries are frequent, build/retain an index over the attachment and store only the index location/identifier in durable memory.
